name: Chemistry
description: Introductory chemistry covering atomic structure, the periodic table, chemical bonding, reactions, stoichiometry, states of matter, solutions, acids and bases, and basic thermodynamics.

modules.title: Matter & Measurement
modules.objectives: Classify matter by state and properties; apply SI units and significant figures to measurements.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy Chemistry — Introduction to Matter
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Introduction to Matter practice
modules.assignment: Collect five household items and classify each as solid, liquid, or gas. For each, identify one physical property and one chemical property. Express one measurement using correct SI units and significant figures.

modules.title: Atomic Structure
modules.objectives: Identify subatomic particles; calculate atomic number, mass number, and understand isotopes; describe the Bohr model.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Chemistry 2e — Ch 2: Atoms, Molecules, and Ions
modules.assignment: Using the periodic table, complete an atom profile for five different elements: list protons, neutrons (for the most common isotope), electrons, atomic number, and mass number.

modules.title: Electron Configuration
modules.objectives: Write electron configurations using orbitals and quantum numbers; identify valence electrons and predict periodic trends.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Chemistry 2e — Ch 6: Electronic Structure & Periodic Properties
modules.assignment: Write full and abbreviated electron configurations for Na, Cl, Fe, and O. Circle the valence electrons for each and predict which element has the highest electronegativity based on configuration.

modules.title: The Periodic Table
modules.objectives: Describe the organization of the periodic table into periods and groups; distinguish metals, nonmetals, and metalloids; explain trends in atomic radius, electronegativity, and ionization energy.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Chemistry 2e — Ch 6.5: Periodic Variations in Element Properties
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy Chemistry — Periodic Table Overview
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Chemistry #3 — The Periodic Table
modules.assignment: Create a mini periodic table for elements 1–20. For each element, label it as metal, nonmetal, or metalloid and indicate whether atomic radius increases or decreases across the period and down the group.

modules.title: Chemical Bonding
modules.objectives: Distinguish ionic and covalent bonds; draw Lewis structures; apply VSEPR theory to predict molecular shape; determine polarity.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Chemistry 2e — Ch 7: Chemical Bonding & Molecular Geometry
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy Chemistry — Types of Chemical Bonds
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Ionic and Covalent Bonding practice
modules.assignment: Draw Lewis structures for H₂O, NH₃, CO₂, and CH₄. For each, predict the molecular geometry using VSEPR theory and determine whether the molecule is polar or nonpolar.

modules.title: Chemical Nomenclature
modules.objectives: Name and write formulas for ionic compounds, molecular compounds, acids, and polyatomic ions.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Chemistry 2e — Ch 2.6: Molecular and Ionic Compounds
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy Chemistry — Naming Ionic Compounds
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Naming Ionic Compounds practice
modules.assignment: Name the following compounds: NaCl, CaCO₃, HNO₃, SO₂, Fe₂O₃, and NH₄Cl. Then write formulas for: magnesium chloride, sulfuric acid, nitrogen dioxide, and copper(II) sulfate.

modules.title: Chemical Reactions & Balancing
modules.titleZh: 化学反应与方程式配平
modules.objectives: Identify the five types of chemical reactions; balance chemical equations using conservation of mass.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Chemistry 2e — Ch 4: Stoichiometry of Chemical Reactions
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy Chemistry — Balancing Chemical Equations
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Balancing Chemical Equations practice
modules.assignment: Balance the following equations and classify each reaction type: (1) H₂ + O₂ → H₂O, (2) CaCO₃ → CaO + CO₂, (3) Fe + CuSO₄ → FeSO₄ + Cu, (4) NaOH + HCl → NaCl + H₂O, (5) CH₄ + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O.

modules.title: Stoichiometry
modules.objectives: Use the mole concept and molar mass to perform stoichiometric calculations; identify limiting reagents and calculate percent yield.
modules.assignment: Given the reaction 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O: (a) Calculate moles of H₂O produced from 4 mol H₂. (b) If 5 g of H₂ reacts with 32 g of O₂, identify the limiting reagent. (c) Calculate percent yield if only 30 g of H₂O is actually collected.

modules.title: States of Matter & Gas Laws
modules.objectives: Apply kinetic molecular theory to gas behavior; solve problems using Boyle's, Charles's, Avogadro's, and ideal gas laws.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy Chemistry — Ideal Gas Equation
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Chemistry #16 — Ideal Gas Laws
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Ideal Gas Equation practice
modules.assignment: A gas occupies 3.0 L at 100 kPa. (a) Using Boyle's Law, find the new pressure if volume is compressed to 1.5 L. (b) Using Charles's Law, find the new volume at 600 K if originally at 300 K and 2.0 L. (c) Use the ideal gas law to find moles of gas at STP if volume is 22.4 L.

modules.title: Solutions
modules.objectives: Describe factors affecting solubility; calculate solution concentration using molarity; perform dilution calculations; explain colligative properties.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Chemistry 2e — Ch 11: Solutions and Colloids
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy Chemistry — Solutions and Solubility
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Molarity and Dilution practice
modules.assignment: Calculate the molarity of a solution made by dissolving 58.5 g of NaCl in 1.0 L of water. Then determine the volume of 2.0 M HCl needed to prepare 500 mL of 0.5 M HCl using the dilution formula.

modules.title: Acids, Bases & pH
modules.objectives: Apply Arrhenius and Brønsted-Lowry definitions; calculate pH and pOH; describe neutralization reactions and titration.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy Chemistry — Arrhenius Acids and Bases
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Brønsted-Lowry Acids and Bases practice
modules.assignment: Calculate the pH of: (a) a solution with [H⁺] = 0.001 M, (b) a 0.1 M NaOH solution. Then write the balanced neutralization equation for HCl reacting with NaOH and calculate how many mL of 0.5 M NaOH is needed to neutralize 20 mL of 0.5 M HCl.

modules.title: Thermochemistry
modules.objectives: Distinguish endothermic from exothermic processes; calculate enthalpy changes using calorimetry and Hess's Law.
modules.videoNote: Khan Academy Chemistry — Internal Energy and Enthalpy
modules.assignment: A 50 g sample of water is heated from 20°C to 80°C (specific heat = 4.18 J/g°C). (a) Calculate the heat absorbed. (b) Classify the process as endothermic or exothermic from the water's perspective. (c) Use Hess's Law to calculate ΔH for a given reaction from two provided equations.

modules.title: Reaction Rates & Equilibrium
modules.objectives: Identify factors that affect reaction rates; apply Le Chatelier's principle; interpret equilibrium constants.
modules.readingNote: OpenStax Chemistry 2e — Ch 13: Fundamental Equilibrium Concepts
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Rates of Reaction practice
modules.assignment: For the equilibrium N₂ + 3H₂ ⇌ 2NH₃ (exothermic): predict the shift when (a) pressure increases, (b) temperature increases, (c) H₂ is added, and (d) NH₃ is removed. Write the equilibrium expression Kc for this reaction.

modules.title: Nuclear Chemistry & Organic Introduction
modules.titleZh: 核化学与有机化学入门
modules.objectives: Describe radioactive decay types and calculate half-life; distinguish fission from fusion; identify basic organic functional groups.
modules.assignment: A radioactive isotope has a half-life of 10 years. Starting with 80 g: (a) How much remains after 30 years? (b) Write the nuclear equation for alpha decay of Ra-226. (c) Identify the functional group in ethanol (CH₃CH₂OH) and name the class of organic compound.

questions.question: Which of the following is a physical change?
The atomic number of an element equals:
options: The number of electrons plus neutrons

questions.question: Isotopes of the same element have the same number of protons but differ in their number of:
How many valence electrons does a sulfur (Group 16) atom have?
Which trend is correct for atomic radius moving left to right across a period?
An ionic bond forms between:
options: A metal and a nonmetal transferring electrons
questions.answer: A metal and a nonmetal transferring electrons

questions.question: The molecular geometry of water (H₂O) is:
What is the correct name for the compound NaCl?
In the reaction 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O, the coefficient in front of H₂O is:
One mole of any substance contains approximately:
The molar mass of H₂O is approximately:
The SI unit of temperature used in gas law calculations is:
Explain the difference between a physical property and a chemical property. Give one example of each for iron.
questions.answer: A physical property (e.g., density, melting point) can be observed without changing composition; a chemical property (e.g., rusting/oxidation) describes how a substance reacts and changes identity.

questions.question: Draw the Lewis structure for CO₂ and predict its molecular shape. Is the molecule polar or nonpolar? Explain.
questions.answer: CO₂ has two double bonds between C and each O, linear geometry (VSEPR), and is nonpolar because the bond dipoles cancel due to symmetry.

questions.question: Balance the following equation and identify its reaction type: CH₄ + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O. Then explain what conservation of mass means in the context of this reaction.
questions.answer: Balanced: CH₄ + 2O₂ → CO₂ + 2H₂O. Combustion reaction. Conservation of mass means the number of atoms of each element is equal on both sides; matter is neither created nor destroyed.

questions.question: According to Boyle's Law, if the pressure of a gas doubles at constant temperature, the volume:
Molarity is defined as:
options: Moles of solute per kilogram of solvent
Moles of solute per liter of solution
Grams of solute per liter of solvent
Moles of solvent per liter of solution
questions.answer: Moles of solute per liter of solution

questions.question: A solution with pH = 3 is:
According to Brønsted-Lowry theory, an acid is a:
An endothermic reaction:
Hess's Law states that:
options: Enthalpy depends only on the path taken
The total enthalpy change is independent of the reaction pathway
Only exothermic reactions obey conservation of energy
questions.answer: The total enthalpy change is independent of the reaction pathway

questions.question: Increasing the concentration of reactants generally:
options: Has no effect on reaction rate

questions.question: Le Chatelier's principle predicts that adding more product to an equilibrium system will shift the reaction:
Alpha (α) particles consist of:
Nuclear fission involves:
options: Combining small nuclei to form a larger nucleus
Splitting a heavy nucleus into smaller fragments
The emission of an electron from the nucleus
questions.answer: Splitting a heavy nucleus into smaller fragments

questions.question: After two half-lives, how much of an original radioactive sample remains?
The functional group –OH (hydroxyl) is characteristic of:
Colligative properties depend on:
options: The chemical identity of the solute
The number of solute particles in solution
The temperature of the solvent only
The charge of the solute ions only
questions.answer: The number of solute particles in solution

questions.question: In a calorimetry experiment, q = mcΔT. If m = 100 g, c = 4.18 J/g°C, and ΔT = 5°C, what is q?
Which gas law combines Boyle's, Charles's, and Avogadro's laws into a single equation?
The limiting reagent in a chemical reaction is:
options: The reagent present in the largest amount
The reagent that is completely consumed first and limits product formation
The reactant with the highest molar mass
questions.answer: The reagent that is completely consumed first and limits product formation

questions.question: Explain the concept of dynamic equilibrium. Use the reaction H₂ + I₂ ⇌ 2HI as an example and describe what happens at the molecular level when equilibrium is reached.
questions.answer: At dynamic equilibrium, the forward and reverse reactions occur at equal rates so there is no net change in concentrations. For H₂ + I₂ ⇌ 2HI, HI molecules are forming and decomposing at the same rate; the system appears static but reactions continue at the molecular level.

questions.question: A student dissolves 117 g of NaCl (molar mass = 58.5 g/mol) in enough water to make 2.0 L of solution. Calculate the molarity of the solution and describe how you would further dilute it to 0.5 M.
questions.answer: Moles of NaCl = 117 / 58.5 = 2.0 mol. Molarity = 2.0 mol / 2.0 L = 1.0 M. To dilute to 0.5 M: use M₁V₁ = M₂V₂; take 500 mL of the 1.0 M solution and add water to make 1.0 L total.

questions.question: Compare alpha, beta, and gamma radiation in terms of charge, mass, and penetrating power. Which type is most dangerous if ingested and why?
questions.answer: Alpha (α): +2 charge, mass ≈ 4 amu, low penetration (stopped by paper or skin). Beta (β): –1 charge, mass ≈ 0, moderate penetration (stopped by thin metal). Gamma (γ): no charge, no mass, highest penetration (requires lead/thick concrete). Alpha radiation is most dangerous if ingested because its high ionizing power causes significant biological damage at close range inside tissue.

questions.question: Electronegativity generally increases:
options: Down a group and left to right across a period
Down a group and right to left across a period
Up a group and left to right across a period
Up a group and right to left across a period
questions.answer: Up a group and left to right across a period

quizQuestions.question: Which state of matter has a definite volume but no definite shape?
The SI base unit for mass is:
Which of the following is a chemical property?
The number 0.00340 expressed in scientific notation is:
How many significant figures are in the measurement 4.050 g?
The particle in an atom with no electrical charge is the:
The mass number of an atom equals:
Carbon-12 and Carbon-14 are:
In the Bohr model, electrons orbit the nucleus in:
An element with atomic number 17 has how many protons?
The Aufbau principle states that electrons fill orbitals:
options: Starting from the highest energy level
Starting from the lowest available energy level
Two at a time in separate orbitals first
quizQuestions.answer: Starting from the lowest available energy level

quizQuestions.question: How many electrons can a 2p subshell hold?
The electron configuration of oxygen (atomic number 8) is:
Hund's rule states that electrons in the same subshell:
options: Pair up as quickly as possible
Occupy separate orbitals with parallel spins before pairing
quizQuestions.answer: Occupy separate orbitals with parallel spins before pairing

quizQuestions.question: The Pauli exclusion principle states that no two electrons in an atom can have:
options: The same set of all four quantum numbers
quizQuestions.answer: The same set of all four quantum numbers

quizQuestions.question: Elements in the same group of the periodic table have the same number of:
Metalloids (semimetals) are found:
options: Far left of the periodic table
Far right of the periodic table
Along the staircase boundary between metals and nonmetals
In the middle transition metals only
quizQuestions.answer: Along the staircase boundary between metals and nonmetals

quizQuestions.question: First ionization energy generally increases:
options: Left to right across a period
Right to left across a period
Moving to the center of a period
quizQuestions.answer: Left to right across a period

quizQuestions.question: The halogen group elements are found in group:
Noble gases are chemically unreactive because they:
options: Have a full outer valence shell of electrons
quizQuestions.answer: Have a full outer valence shell of electrons

quizQuestions.question: In an ionic bond, electrons are:
A Lewis structure shows:
options: The three-dimensional shape of a molecule
Valence electrons as dots around the atomic symbol
quizQuestions.answer: Valence electrons as dots around the atomic symbol

quizQuestions.question: VSEPR theory predicts molecular shape based on:
options: Atomic mass of the central atom
Repulsion between electron pairs around the central atom
quizQuestions.answer: Repulsion between electron pairs around the central atom

quizQuestions.question: A polar covalent bond forms when:
options: Electrons are shared unequally due to different electronegativities
quizQuestions.answer: Electrons are shared unequally due to different electronegativities

quizQuestions.question: The shape of a molecule with 4 bonding pairs and 0 lone pairs around the central atom is:
The compound Fe₂O₃ is named:
The formula for calcium nitrate is:
The prefix 'tetra-' in covalent nomenclature indicates:
Acids that contain oxygen are called:
The name of HCl when dissolved in water is:
A synthesis reaction has the general form:
In a combustion reaction, a hydrocarbon reacts with O₂ to produce:
When balancing a chemical equation, you may only change:
In a double-displacement reaction, two ionic compounds exchange:
The law that explains why equations must be balanced is:
One mole of carbon-12 has a mass of:
How many moles are in 36 g of H₂O (molar mass = 18 g/mol)?
Mole ratios in stoichiometry come from:
options: Atomic masses on the periodic table
Coefficients in the balanced chemical equation
The number of electrons in each atom
quizQuestions.answer: Coefficients in the balanced chemical equation

quizQuestions.question: The limiting reagent is the reactant that:
options: Is present in the greatest moles
Is completely used up first and determines maximum product
Requires the most energy to react
quizQuestions.answer: Is completely used up first and determines maximum product

quizQuestions.question: Percent yield is calculated as:
According to kinetic molecular theory, gas particles:
options: Have strong attractive forces between them
Occupy most of the container volume

quizQuestions.question: Boyle's Law states that at constant temperature, pressure and volume are:
Charles's Law states that at constant pressure, volume and temperature are:
options: Inversely related by a squared factor

quizQuestions.question: The ideal gas law is expressed as:
STP (Standard Temperature and Pressure) refers to:
Solubility of most solid solutes in water increases with:
A solution is 2.0 M NaCl. This means:
options: 2.0 mol NaCl per liter of solution
2.0 mol NaCl per kg of solvent
2.0 g NaCl per kg of solvent
quizQuestions.answer: 2.0 mol NaCl per liter of solution

quizQuestions.question: The dilution equation is:
Boiling point elevation is an example of a:
"Like dissolves like" means:
options: Polar solvents dissolve polar solutes; nonpolar solvents dissolve nonpolar solutes
All solutes dissolve in any solvent
Solid solutes dissolve only in solid solvents
quizQuestions.answer: Polar solvents dissolve polar solutes; nonpolar solvents dissolve nonpolar solutes

quizQuestions.question: According to the Arrhenius definition, a base is a substance that produces ______ ions in water.
A solution with pH = 7 is:
In a neutralization reaction, an acid reacts with a base to form:
Titration is used to determine:
options: The unknown concentration of an acid or base
The boiling point of a solution
The molar mass of a solid
quizQuestions.answer: The unknown concentration of an acid or base

quizQuestions.question: If [H⁺] = 1 × 10⁻⁴ M, the pH is:
Enthalpy (H) is a measure of:
options: The heat content of a system at constant pressure
quizQuestions.answer: The heat content of a system at constant pressure

quizQuestions.question: An exothermic reaction has a ΔH value that is:
Calorimetry measures:
options: Heat exchanged during a chemical or physical process
quizQuestions.answer: Heat exchanged during a chemical or physical process

quizQuestions.question: The specific heat of water is 4.18 J/g°C. This means:
options: It takes 4.18 kJ to heat 1 kg of water 1°C
It takes 4.18 J to raise 1 g of water by 1°C
quizQuestions.answer: It takes 4.18 J to raise 1 g of water by 1°C

quizQuestions.question: Hess's Law allows us to calculate ΔH by:
options: Adding ΔH values of individual steps in a reaction pathway
quizQuestions.answer: Adding ΔH values of individual steps in a reaction pathway

quizQuestions.question: Increasing temperature generally increases the reaction rate because:
options: It gives more particles enough energy to overcome the activation energy barrier
It changes the equilibrium constant only
quizQuestions.answer: It gives more particles enough energy to overcome the activation energy barrier

quizQuestions.question: A catalyst speeds up a reaction by:
options: Providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy
quizQuestions.answer: Providing an alternative pathway with lower activation energy

quizQuestions.question: Le Chatelier's principle states that if a stress is applied to a system at equilibrium, the system will:
options: Reach a new equilibrium favoring the stress direction
Shift to relieve the stress and reach a new equilibrium
Increase the rate of both forward and reverse reactions equally forever
quizQuestions.answer: Shift to relieve the stress and reach a new equilibrium

quizQuestions.question: For the reaction A + B ⇌ C + D, the equilibrium constant expression Kc is:
If Kc >> 1, the equilibrium strongly favors:
Beta (β) decay occurs when:
options: A nucleus emits an alpha particle
A neutron converts to a proton and emits an electron
A nucleus absorbs a gamma ray
quizQuestions.answer: A neutron converts to a proton and emits an electron

quizQuestions.question: The half-life of a radioactive isotope is:
options: The time for half the atoms to gain a proton
The time for half of the radioactive atoms to decay
The half of its atomic mass
The time for its radiation to decrease by 100%
quizQuestions.answer: The time for half of the radioactive atoms to decay

quizQuestions.question: Nuclear fusion releases energy by:
options: Combining light nuclei (such as hydrogen) into a heavier nucleus
Emitting gamma rays from a heavy atom
quizQuestions.answer: Combining light nuclei (such as hydrogen) into a heavier nucleus

quizQuestions.question: The simplest organic compound class, containing only C–C and C–H single bonds, is:
If a radioactive sample starts with 100 g and has a half-life of 5 years, how much remains after 15 years?
